---
title: "Reports"
sidebar: arcs02
filters:
  - webr
webr:
  cell-options:
    reset-button: true

---

::: {.callout-note}
## By the end of this section, you should be able to...
- Convert a plain text file into a Quarto document (`.qmd`)
- Render a `.qmd` file to HTML and understand what "localhost" means
- Embed R code chunks into a Quarto document with correct syntax
- Use `fs::dir_tree()` to automatically generate and annotate a project
  structure in your README
- Re-render a document to reflect updated content
- Create a Quarto document using a journal template (PLOS)
- Run a gene variability analysis and interpret summary statistics
- Generate and save a publication-quality expression heatmap
:::

---

## Why Reports?

Quarto documents let you combine code, results, and narrative text in a
single file that can be rendered to HTML, PDF, or Word. This means your
analysis and its documentation live together — when your code changes, your
report updates automatically. This is a core reproducibility practice.

---

## Convert README.txt to a Quarto Document

1. Navigate to your `README.txt` file in your ARCS project folder
   (PC: Windows Explorer; Mac: Finder)

2. Right click on the file and select **Rename**

3. Change the file extension from `.txt` to `.qmd`
   (Mac: type `.qmd` after `README`)

4. Accept the file extension change and save the file

5. Go back to Positron and open your `README.qmd` file

6. Add triple backticks ( ` ``` ` ) before and after your README's file
   structure tree to format it as a code block

7. In Positron, click the **Render** button to render your document

8. A browser window should open and display your `README.qmd` file as HTML

::: {.callout-note}
## What is localhost?
Notice that the browser window's address starts with `localhost` followed
by a port number. This means your computer is serving your `.qmd` file
locally to itself — the file is not on the internet, it is being previewed
from your own machine.
:::

---

## Automate README.qmd Updates

Instead of manually updating your README every time your project structure
changes, we can write an R code chunk that generates the file tree
automatically each time you render.

1. Check whether you have the `fs` package installed:

```r
library(fs)
```

If you get an error, install it first:

```r
install.packages("fs")
```

2. Copy the script below:

```r
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| results: asis

library(fs)

# Get the actual structure
tree_output <- capture.output(dir_tree(".", recurse = 2))

# Define comments for specific files/folders
comments <- list(
  "raw_data"       = " (original data goes here)",
  "processed_data" = " (clean data goes here)",
  "scripts"        = " (code goes here)",
  "outputs"        = " (results and charts go here)",
  "README.qmd"     = " (project documentation)"
)

# Add comments to matching lines
for(item in seq_along(tree_output)) {
  line <- tree_output[item]
  for(name in names(comments)) {
    if(grepl(name, line)) {
      tree_output[item] <- paste0(line, comments[[name]])
      break
    }
  }
}

# Print the result
cat("```\n")
cat(paste(tree_output, collapse = "\n"))
cat("\n```\n")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What this script does
- `dir_tree()` generates a visual tree of your project folder up to
  2 levels deep
- `capture.output()` stores that tree as a character vector
- The `comments` list defines annotations to add next to specific
  folders or files
- The `for` loop finds matching lines and appends the annotations
- `cat()` prints the result as a formatted code block in your rendered
  document
:::

3. Paste this script into your `README.qmd` file in between the triple
   backticks where your file tree currently appears

4. Next to the opening triple backticks, add `{r}` to make it an R code
   chunk — it should look like this: ` ```{r} `

5. Save your `README.qmd` file

6. Click the **Render** button in Positron

7. Go back to your browser window to see the result

8. Make any changes you would like to the `comments <- list()` section
   and re-render to see them reflected

---

## Create a Quarto Document for Publishing

In this exercise you will create a publication-quality document using a
PLOS journal template, run a gene variability analysis, and generate a
heatmap figure.

### Setup

1. Check whether you have the following packages installed:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
```

If any are missing, install them in your console:

```r
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("readxl")
install.packages("scales")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Checking installed packages in Positron
You can also check in the **Extensions** pane by searching for the
package name.
:::

### Install the PLOS Template

2. In your **Terminal** tab, run the following commands one at a time:

```bash
quarto add quarto-journals/plos
```

```bash
quarto use template quarto-journals/plos
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What this does
This installs a Quarto extension that formats your document to match
PLOS journal submission requirements — including layout, fonts, and
figure formatting. This is the same approach used for many other journal
templates available through `quarto-journals`.
:::

### Gene Variability Analysis

3. Copy the code below and paste it into your `publication.qmd` file
   underneath the heading `## Gene Variability Analysis`.
   Start your code block with ` ```{r} ` and close it with ` ``` `.

::: {.callout-note}
## About the `#|` lines
These are chunk options — they tell Quarto how to handle the output
when this code runs inside a `.qmd` file. `message: false` suppresses
package loading messages, and `warning: false` suppresses warnings.
:::

```r
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Prepare data - rename the first column and use both ID columns
gene_data_long <- gene_data %>%
  rename(ensembl_id = `...1`) %>%
  filter(!is.na(gene_symbol), gene_symbol != "") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(ensembl_id, gene_symbol),
               names_to = "sample",
               values_to = "expression")

# Calculate variance for each gene across all samples
gene_variability <- gene_data_long %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol, ensembl_id) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_expression        = mean(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    variance               = var(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    coefficient_of_variation = sd(expression, na.rm = TRUE) / mean_expression,
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

# Identify top 15 most variable genes
top_genes <- gene_variability %>%
  arrange(desc(variance)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

# Display summary statistics
cat("Total genes analyzed:", nrow(gene_variability), "\n")
cat("Mean variance across all genes:",
    round(mean(gene_variability$variance, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "\n")
cat("Most variable gene:", top_genes$gene_symbol[1],
    "with variance =", round(top_genes$variance[1], 2), "\n")

# Show the top 10 most variable genes
cat("\nTop 10 most variable genes:\n")
print(top_genes %>%
        select(gene_symbol, variance) %>%
        slice_head(n = 10) %>%
        mutate(variance = round(variance, 2)))
```

### Data Visualization

4. Copy the code below and paste it into your `publication.qmd` file
   underneath the heading `## Data Visualization`.
   Start your code block with ` ```{r} ` and close it with ` ``` `.

```r
#| fig-cap: "Expression heatmap showing scaled expression levels of the
#|   15 most variable genes across all samples. Blue indicates
#|   below-average expression, white indicates average expression,
#|   and red indicates above-average expression for each gene."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

# Prepare heatmap data
heatmap_data <- gene_data_long %>%
  filter(gene_symbol %in% top_genes$gene_symbol) %>%
  left_join(top_genes %>% select(gene_symbol, variance),
            by = "gene_symbol") %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol) %>%
  mutate(scaled_expr = scale(expression)[, 1]) %>%
  ungroup()

# Create heatmap visualization
expression_heatmap <- heatmap_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sample,
             y = reorder(gene_symbol, variance),
             fill = scaled_expr)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low   = "blue",
    mid   = "white",
    high  = "red",
    name  = "Scaled\nExpression",
    limits = c(-3, 3),
    oob   = scales::squish
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Expression Variability Heatmap: Top 15 Most Variable Genes",
    subtitle = "Genes ordered by variance (highest at top)",
    x        = "Sample ID",
    y        = "Gene Symbol"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 10, face = "italic"),
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid   = element_blank()
  )

print(expression_heatmap)

# Save high-resolution figure
ggsave(paste0("outputs/", timestamp, "_expression_heatmap.png"),
       plot   = expression_heatmap,
       width  = 10,
       height = 6,
       dpi    = 300)
```

5. Render your `publication.qmd` document to see your PLOS-formatted
   article

::: {.callout-tip}
## Saving your figure
Notice the `ggsave()` call at the end of the visualization block. This
saves a high-resolution `.png` to your `outputs/` folder with a
timestamp in the filename — the same automated file naming pattern
introduced in the Project Management section.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note}
## Can You...?
- ✓ Convert a `.txt` file to a `.qmd` file and render it to HTML?
- ✓ Explain what `localhost` means when previewing a rendered document?
- ✓ Add an R code chunk to a `.qmd` file with correct syntax?
- ✓ Use `dir_tree()` and `capture.output()` to auto-generate a project
  tree in your README?
- ✓ Modify the `comments` list and re-render to see updated annotations?
- ✓ Install and apply a Quarto journal template from the terminal?
- ✓ Explain what the gene variability analysis is doing at a high level?
- ✓ Render a publication-quality heatmap and save it to your `outputs/`
  folder?
:::
